ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for tiramisu as a prepared dessert food item; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Tiramisu Rich Chocolate is approximately 120 g, equivalent to one standard dessert slice or individual portion cup as commonly served in Singapore cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Tiramisu — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tiramisu (record G00010032). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (USDA SR Legacy, Tiramisu, NDB No. 19921) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; the USDA value of approximately 13.5 g/100g saturated fat for tiramisu is used as a cross-reference estimate and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tiramisu Rich Chocolate (93% cocoa variant). GI of 62 is estimated using the closest analogous food: tiramisu (standard preparation) referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which reports GI values for cream- and sugar-based layered desserts in the medium range (55–70). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 29.44 × 1.2) / 100 = 21.9 g GL per 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (18.7 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (0.6 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and promotes weight gain, independently elevating blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Tiramisu Rich Chocolate due to its high sugar load (18.7 g/100g) and low fibre (0.6 g/100g), which promote insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain that independently raise blood pressure, even though sodium at 173 mg/100g is moderate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 18.7 g/100g combined with low dietary fibre (0.6 g/100g) and a medium glycemic index (estimated GI 62) produces rapid postprandial glucose excursions; high saturated fat (~13.5 g/100g) further worsens insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Tiramisu Rich Chocolate or limit intake to very small occasional portions, as the high sugar content (18.7 g/100g), low fibre (0.6 g/100g), estimated GI of 62, and high saturated fat (~13.5 g/100g) combine to cause rapid blood glucose spikes and worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (23.86 g/100g) with estimated saturated fat of ~13.5 g/100g and high cholesterol (121 mg/100g) significantly elevates LDL cholesterol risk; high sugar load further promotes triglyceride elevation and endothelial inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Tiramisu Rich Chocolate due to its high saturated fat (~13.5 g/100g), high cholesterol (121 mg/100g), and high sugar (18.7 g/100g), which collectively raise LDL cholesterol, promote hypertriglyceridaemia, and increase cardiovascular event risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~13.5 g/100g) from mascarpone and cream is strongly pro-inflammatory; high sugar (18.7 g/100g) promotes inflammatory mediator production.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Tiramisu Rich Chocolate, as its high saturated fat (~13.5 g/100g) and high sugar (18.7 g/100g) promote systemic inflammation and may worsen joint symptoms, outweighing any minor anti-inflammatory benefit from cocoa polyphenols.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tiramisu Rich Chocolate is a Singapore-market variant of the classic Italian tiramisu dessert, distinguished by the use of 93% cocoa dark chocolate alongside dairy milk, giving it an intensely rich and bittersweet chocolate character. The dessert is constructed in layers: espresso-soaked ladyfinger biscuits (savoiardi) are alternated with a creamy mascarpone-based filling enriched with eggs, sugar, and dairy milk, then finished with a generous dusting or layer of high-cocoa dark chocolate. The texture is soft, moist, and indulgent, with a contrast between the slightly firm biscuit layers and the smooth, airy cream. Per 100 g, it delivers 353 kcal, 23.86 g fat (estimated saturated fat ~13.5 g), 29.44 g carbohydrates, 18.7 g sugar, 5.65 g protein, 0.6 g dietary fibre, and 173 mg sodium. Cholesterol is notably high at 121 mg/100g. The product contains dairy and soy allergens and is not suitable for vegans. Clinically, this dessert is high concern for Type 2 Diabetes (high sugar, low fibre, medium GI ~62), hypertension (high sugar drives insulin-mediated sodium retention), and cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat and cholesterol). It is best consumed as a rare occasional treat in very small portions by individuals without metabolic or cardiovascular conditions.
common_user_queries: Can I eat tiramisu if I have high blood pressure?
What is the fat content of tiramisu?
Is tiramisu suitable for heart patients?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010032) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. Saturated fat is cross-referenced from USDA SR Legacy tiramisu entry and documented as an estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed source, clearly documented. All other fields are derived from approved sources or defensibly estimated.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (13.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Tiramisu, NDB No. 19921) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is documented as an adapted estimate. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for these nutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010032) or the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy tiramisu entry (NDB No. 19921) as of 2026, and no other approved source provides values for this specific preparation: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g (fatty acid fractions not separately reported in HPB or USDA SR Legacy for this tiramisu entry); vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg (micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA SR Legacy tiramisu entry does not include a complete vitamin panel for this food); phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg (trace minerals not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry); water_g (moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry). Sources consulted and confirmed unavailable: HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/, accessed 2026-04-16) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/, accessed 2026-04-16). 3. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on tiramisu (standard preparation) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement exists for this specific 93% cocoa variant. Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (62 × 29.44 × 1.2) / 100 = 21.9. 4. The product description references a sugar-free sweetener option currently out of stock; nutrient values reflect the standard cane sugar formulation. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; the product contains dairy and soy as declared allergens. 6. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered.
